author: @walkerig1
text: The Bronze age began with Arsenical Bronze at least 2000 years before Tin bronze with Arsenical Bronze tools found in Egypt 2000 years before the Tin Bronze age. There is evidence of Arsenical Bronze ores of Copper with Arsenic were both present and those being preferred ores for tools. And for their different colour in some tasks.

author: @lagunafishing
text: It was called the Bronze Age not the copper age, but we know what you mean. Interesting that copper can produce tube drill holes in hard granite using an abrasive slurry made of fine carborundum/ sand and water. It is also thought that a copper cyanide alloy tooling was employed 5,000+ years ago in Egypt - 3,000 years before the Pharos discovered the pyramids. Copper has certainly been used by man a hell of a lot longer than most people realise.

author: @cosmicseekin
text: Domestic Nuclear Battery -  OK :-)   I have no idea if this is already done on a workable scale.  My invention is:-  Based on the Principal of a Diamond Battery.  Scale the whole concept up to a Domestic Energy source.  The fuel would be spent nuclear reactor fuel which is presently put in containers in massive water ponds.  Take a reasonable piece of that decaying fuel.  Encase it in a Glass Substrate and encase that in a small energy conversion box.  The heat/energy from the Radio Active decay can be used to charge batteries to run a house.  The whole thing could be suitably shielded in underground chambers.  When the energy generation drops off after a couple of years, the Glass Capsule would be replaced.  What do you think of this ?

replies.author: @petevenuti7355
replies.text:
You describe a RTG, there isn't enough heat unless you enriched the waste again. 
  If you made the shielding out of the right semiconductors you might be able to make a large betacvoltaic battery , the wattage out would be too small to be of practical use with the grid, or even a house,.
 but such a battery could theoretically last centuries powering small electronics

replies.author: @cosmicseekin
replies.text: ​@petevenuti7355 Looking at the radioisotope therrmoelectric generator (RTG, RITEG) I have concluded that it is not what I was imagining.  The nuclear material does not only give off heat.  I was more interested in the radiation and taking that directly to electrical energy, rather than going to heat that then has to be turned into electricity.  Diamond batteries do not use heat to drive the electrical output which is why I thought scaling that up would be a long term battery supplying energy for domestic use.

replies.author: @petevenuti7355
replies.text:
@cosmicseekin so like a  betacvoltaic. The thing is, well , think of it like counts per second per cm², now how many photons per second hit a solar panel per çm²? The total energy available is actually much less.
  Just back of the napkin and numbers from Google, 
Pure U238 should put out 3,000,000 particles a second in all directions.
A sphere with 1cm² area is about 0.09cm³ , based on the density of U238 that's about 0.0072 mole or 21,600 particles a second per cm² the sun about 100000000000000000 photons per cm² hit earth per second, ok yes the radiation from uranium has about 10 million times as much energy per particle but that's still 1/10,000 total what you would get with a solar panel...
Yes these are rough numbers but still looks like trying to use solar panels with moonlight.
Even if you figured the particles from u238 are +2 charge, and would attract 43,200 electrons per second to wherever they got absorbed, compare that to how many electrons in a culumb of electricity... Small
You have the additional problem of the thickness of material for adsorbing high energy particles compared with it's own resistance, also you are in no way dealing with pure u238 so....
  You ever look up the actual output in watts of that diamond battery?

author: @chemistryofquestionablequa6252
text: Copper is my favorite element, it’s got so many uses and the colors of it’s salts in chemistry are awesome.

author: @gerryjamesedwards1227
text: I live not far from the huge ancient copper mine at the Great Orme. Most of the copper extracted from there was extracted with antlers, which amazes me. They find the worn stubs everywhere down there and are discovering more tunnels down there all the time. Just yesterday I was watching a Norwegian geologist's video on porphyry deposits, where most metals such as copper are found. It's a fascinating process and they are still the subjects of searches spending huge  chunks of exploration budget as more copper is always needed.

replies.author: @purvel
replies.text: Interesting! Could you link to the video?

replies.author: @philip5940
replies.text: But is more copper really needed . It's no longer necessary in plumbing and best avoided in pronounced sub zero climates . For electrical connections and circuitry , it's still the all 'round best . Aluminium can work as a replacement but caused too many housefires in late seventies and was abandoned for domestic wiring .

replies.author: @eone2345
replies.text: @philip5940 copper still has it's use especially it's heat transfer properties is much better than aluminium
